architecture.summary: SparseRM extracts preference-relevant features from LLM intermediate representations using Sparse Autoencoders (SAE). The system identifies salient latent directions in SAE decompositions by comparing activation frequencies between preferred and dispreferred samples, projects input representations onto these directions via inner products, concatenates the projection vectors into a preference-aware vector, and trains a single-layer MLP reward head on these vectors to predict preference scores. The mechanism operates on frozen LLM representations; only the SAE and reward head are trained.
trained_components: sparse autoencoder (applied to intermediate layer representations)

biblio.title: SparseRM: A Lightweight Preference Modeling with Sparse Autoencoder

claims.statement: SparseRM achieves the highest accuracy on TruthfulQA and outperforms most baselines on SafeRLHF and Red-Teaming while using less than 1% of trainable parameters
On Gemma-2-9B-it with TruthfulQA MC1, SparseRM achieves 65.2% accuracy
On Gemma-2-9B-it alignment task, SparseRM achieves 79.9% accuracy on SafeRLHF

SparseRM achieves strong generalization across distribution shifts between supervised preference pairs and model-generated data, suggesting that extracted subspace captures fundamental, preference-relevant features that remain stable regardless of input data distribution changes

Average cosine similarity between generated and training data is significantly lower in dense space (DenseRM) compared to sparse space (SparseRM), indicating more substantial distributional shift in dense representations
Margin loss consistently outperforms binary cross-entropy (BCE) loss and Bradley-Terry (BT) loss across all three datasets in reward modeling

Directly using SAE latent activation values as input to reward head results in limited performance due to low representational capacity of sparse vectors; computing inner products between intermediate representations and selected latent directions produces better preference representation

SAE-extracted sparse representations exhibit clearer separation between positive and negative responses compared to dense representations, and SparseRM more reliably identifies and filters out noisy samples than DenseRM
